meta.title: Connor Douglas
meta.description: PhD candidate at NYU Stern studying machine learning, experimentation, personalization, and markets.

quick.label: Research
quick.value: Data-driven Decision Making • Causal inference • Personalization • Markets

quick.label: Bio
quick.value: I'm a PhD candidate in Information Systems at NYU Stern, in the Technology, Operations, and Statistics department. I love all things at the intersection of computer science, economics, and math.

research.intro:
My research lies at the intersection of machine learning and economics, where I am especially interested in algorithmic decision making. I study the design and evaluation of these systems, with methodological interests largely in adaptive experimentation and causal inference. More substantively, I am especially interested in settings applied to personalization, markets, and language models.

I'm passionate about advancing a notion of algorithmic economics which treats algorithms as imperfect decision makers and studies the quality of these decisions and their downstream effects on interactive or social systems (much like the behavioral economics movement). I believe this is vital not only for improving the performance of algorithmic decision makers in context, but also for studying the safety implications of algorithmic proliferation.

cv.intro: Download my CV or connect on LinkedIn for additional information.

hobbies.text: In free time, some of my biggest hobbies are snowboarding, hiking, drawing, and exploring the NYC food scene. Last year I ran the Boston Marathon (on team BMC) and loaded up on caffeine to do the Badwater Basin to Mt. Whitney duathlon (bike + hike). I've been fortunate to spend a lot of time hiking in the White Mountains (I'm currently at 44/48 4000 footers) and Sierra Nevada, having done both the John Muir Trail and High Sierra Trail. This year, I'm focusing on getting back into drawing, watercoloring, and photography.
